source_metadata.title_raw: Robert W. Schultz, Esq. - A-19-185 - September 12, 2019 - Los Gatos

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Robert W. Schultz, Esq. 
Town Attorney of Los Gatos 
110 E. Main St. 
Los Gatos, CA 95030 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-185 
Dear Mr. Schultz: 
September 12, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Town of Los Gatos 
Councilmembers Steve Leonardis, Marico Sayoc, Barbara Spector, and Rob Rennie regarding the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. 
Do Councilmembers Steve Leonardis, Marico Sayoc and Barbara Spector have a 
disqualifying conflict of interest prohibiting them from taking part in decisions regarding a Mills 
Act Program that would set forth the process and criteria for designation of real property as 
historical properties if they own real property designated as historical property? 
2. 
Does Councilmember Rob Rennie have a disqualifying conflict of interest prohibiting him 
from taking part in decisions regarding a Mills Act Program that would set forth the process and 
criteria for designation ofreal property as historical properties if he owns real property in a 
historical district and within 250 feet of a historical property? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-19-185 
Page No. 2 
3. 
If the Councilmembers are disqualified, may the Town of Los Gatos invoke the "legally 
required participation" exception with respect to the decision-making process on whether to adopt a 
Mills Act Program? 
· 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. 
Yes. It is reasonably foreseeable that the Councilmembers' respective real property will be 
materially affected by participating in the decision-making process. 
2. 
Yes. You have not presented clear and convincing evidence to rebut the presumption that 
Councilmember Rennie has a disqualifying conflict of interest 
3. 
Yes. Given that a quorum of financially disinterested councilmembers cannot be convened, 
the Town of Los Gatos may invoke the ''legally required participation" exception in order to 
facilitate the city council's decision-making process on whether to adopt a Mills Act Program. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
In 1972, California enacted the Mills Act, an economic incentive program to encourage the 
preservation of historic buildings. (Sections 50280-50290.) The Mills Act grants local governments 
the authority to establish, administer, and implement the Mills Act Historic Preservation Tax 
Abatement Programs. Cities and counties with a Mills Act Program can enter into contracts with 
owners of qualified historic properties who actively participate in the restoration and maintenance 
of their historic properties while receiving property tax relief. Each local government establishes its 
own criteria for evaluating applications and determines how many contracts will be allowed within 
its jurisdiction. 
To establish a Mills Act Program, the Council would need to adopt an enabling ordinance. 
The Town currently defines a historic structure as being built prior to 1941. A Mills Act ordinance 
would need to establish criteria to determine which historical structures may be eligible under the 
Mills Act and determine how many contracts they will allow annually. The amount of tax 
savings varies but may result in up to 60 percent property tax savings per year. In addition, a 
research study looked at the value of the Mills Act on historical property and concluded that they 
receive a 16% premium over similar houses in the same neighborhood and homebuyers are 
willing to pay a significant premium for a historically designated house. The results of the study 
also suggest that a house's value is increased by 3.8% by having a Mills Act historical house 
within 250 ft. 
Councilmembers Steve Leonardis, Marico Sayoc, and Barbara Spector own real property 
defined as historical structures under Town Code. Councilmember Rob Rennie owns real property 
within a historical district and within 250 feet of historical property, but his property is not defined 
as a historical structure under Town Code. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-19-185 
Page No. 3 
Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes, 
participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision which has a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests. 
Section' 87103(b) provides that an official has a financial interest in "[ a ]ny real property in 
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or 
more." Furthermore, Section 87103 states that a public official also has an interest in his or her 
"personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family, also known as the 'personal 
financial effects' rule. "2 
Because the Councilmembers own real estate worth more than two thousand dollars, they 
have real property interests that warrant analysis under the Act's conflict of interest provisions. 
Fores eability: 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the 
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) A 
financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision affects a real property financial 
interest as described in Regulation 18702.2( a)(l )-( 6). Relevant to the facts provided are decisions 
regarding the following: 
(1) Involves the adoption of or amendment to a development plan or criteria applying to the 
parcel; 
(3) Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel. 
Decisions establishing and implementing a Mills Act Program explicitly involve 
Councilmembers Leonardis, Sayoc and Spector's respective real property because a portion of the 
discussion involve the adoption of development criteria in establishing eligibility of their historical 
structures under a Mills Act Program, which may also result in a reduction in taxes for the 
properties. Accordingly, the effect of Mills Act Program-decisions on the Councilmembers' real 
properties, is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable. 
Given that Councilmember Rennie's real property is not considered historical, his house 
would not be explicitly involved in the decision-making process. Thus, as applied to his interest, 
Regulation 18701 (b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." 
2 Effects on an official's personal finances are not considered separately from the effect on an official's interest 
in a business entity or real property. (Regulation 18702.S(c).) Thus, we do not consider the personal financial effects 
rule further. 


Materiality: 
File No. A-19-185 
Page No. 4 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. Under Regulation 18701, the effect 
of a decision on an interest in real property is material whenever the decision: ( 1) involves the 
adoption of or amendment to a development plan or criteria applying to the parcel or (2) would 
impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel. Based upon the 
facts provided, when a property receives a Mills Act designation, it may receive up to a 60 percent 
property tax savings per year. Additionally, you have referenced a study concluding that Mills Act 
properties receive a 16% premium over similar houses in the same neighborhood and homebuyers 
are willing to pay a significant premium for a historically designated house. Thus, it is reasonably 
foreseeable that decisions regarding the Mills Act Program will have a material effect on 
Councilmembers Leonardis, Sayoc, and Spector's respective real property. 
In regard to Councilmember Rennie's real property, Regulation 18702.2 provides that the 
financial effect of the decisions on the official's real property interest is material if the decision 
involves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear 
and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's 
property. 
You indicate that Councilmember Rennie's real property is located within a historical 
district, 250 feet of a historical property. In regard to the potential effect of the decision on the 
property, the only information provided was a reference to a study indicating that a property's value 
is increased by 3.8% by having a Mills Act historical house within 250 ft. Thus, you have not 
presented clear and convincing evidence to rebut the presumption that Councilmember Rennie has a 
disqualifying conflict of interest under Regulation 18702.2. 
Legally Required Part,icipation Exception: 
Under the "legally required participation" exception set forth in Section 87101 and 
Regulation 18705, a public official may participate in the making of a governmental decision being 
financially interested in the decision if the official's participation is legally required for the action or 
decision to be made. 
Regulation 18705( c) requires the "legally required participation" exception to be construed 
narrowly. The exception cannot be invoked to allow a financially interested official to participate in 
a decision if a quorum can be convened of other members of the agency who are not disqualified, 
and participation is required to be limited to the smallest number of officials with a disqualifying 
interest that are "legally required" to make the decision.3 (Regulation 18705(c)(2)-(3).) 
Because the Town's City Council is comprised of five councilmembers, at least three will be 
necessary to form a quorum to decide whether to adopt a Mills Act Program. Councilmembers 
3 As is pertinent to the facts presented, Regulation I 8705(d) defines a "quorum" as "the minimum number of 
members required to conduct business." 


File No. A-19-185 
Page No. 5 
Leonardis, Sayoc, Spector, and Rennie are disqualified from taking part in the decision pursuant to 
Section 87100, leaving less than a quorum of councilmembers available to make that determination. 
Therefore, two of those disqualified Councilmembers will be "legally required" to take part in that 
decision in order to conduct business, and the Town may invoke the exception. 
Accordingly, the Town is authorized to use a random means of selection to select two 
councilmembers among Councilmembers Leonardis, Sayoc, Spector, and Rennie to form a quorum 
with respect to the decisions at issue. (See Regulation 18705(c)(3).) When two of those 
Councilmembers are selected, those Councilmembers are selected for the duration of the 
proceedings in all related matters until their participation is no longer legally required, or the need 
for invoking the exception no longer exists. The selected Councilmembers will be required to state 
the existence of the potential conflict of interest, as specified in Regulation 18705(b ). 
Thus, we conclude that the Act prohibits Councilmembers Leonardis, Sayoc, Spector, and 
Rennie from taking part in that decision, but the Town may invoke the "legally required 
participation" exception to form a quorum of councilmembers with respect to that decision, as 
specified in Regulation 18705. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:sal 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Ryan O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division

QUESTION: 1. 
Do Councilmembers Steve Leonardis, Marico Sayoc and Barbara Spector have a 
disqualifying conflict of interest prohibiting them from taking part in decisions regarding a Mills 
Act Program that would set forth the process and criteria for designation of real property as 
historical properties if they own real property designated as historical property?

from taking part in decisions regarding a Mills Act Program that would set forth the process and 
criteria for designation ofreal property as historical properties if he owns real property in a 
historical district and within 250 feet of a historical property?3. 
If the Councilmembers are disqualified, may the Town of Los Gatos invoke the "legally 
required participation" exception with respect to the decision-making process on whether to adopt a 
Mills Act Program? 
·

CONCLUSION: 1. 
Yes. It is reasonably foreseeable that the Councilmembers' respective real property will be 
materially affected by participating in the decision-making process. 
2. 
Yes. You have not presented clear and convincing evidence to rebut the presumption that 
Councilmember Rennie has a disqualifying conflict of interest 
3. 
Yes. Given that a quorum of financially disinterested councilmembers cannot be convened, 
the Town of Los Gatos may invoke the ''legally required participation" exception in order to 
facilitate the city council's decision-making process on whether to adopt a Mills Act Program.
